Here are further instructions for Christian duties: being like-minded and humble, following the example of the Lord Jesus. Kindness is the rule of Christ’s kingdom, the lesson of his teaching, and a sign of belonging to his family. Several reasons to love one another are mentioned. If you expect or experience God's compassion toward yourselves, be compassionate toward others. It brings ministers joy to see people who are like-minded. Christ came to humble us; let there not be pride among us. We must be strict with our own faults and quick to notice our own shortcomings, but ready to make allowances for others. We must care for others kindly, but not interfere in other people's affairs. Neither inner nor outer peace can be enjoyed without humility.

The example of our Lord Jesus Christ is set before us. We must resemble him in his life if we want to experience the benefit of his death. Consider the two natures of Christ: his Divine nature and his human nature. He, who existed in the form of God, sharing the Divine nature as the eternal and only-begotten Son of God, did not consider it something to be seized as a prize to be equal with God and to receive worship from people. In his human nature, he became like us in all things except sin. Thus, willingly, he lowered himself from the glory he had with the Father before the world began. Christ’s two states, of humiliation and exaltation, are noted. Christ not only took upon himself the likeness and form of a man but of one who was humble; he did not appear in splendor. His whole life was a life of poverty and suffering. But the lowest point was his dying on the cross, the death of a criminal and a slave, exposed to public hatred and scorn. The exaltation was of Christ’s human nature, united with the Divine. At the name of Jesus—not just the sound of the word but the authority of Jesus—everyone should pay solemn respect. It is for the glory of God the Father to confess that Jesus Christ is Lord; because it is his will that everyone should honor the Son as they honor the Father. Here we see motives for self-denying love that nothing else can provide. Do we thus love and obey the Son of God?

We must be diligent in using all the means that lead to our salvation, persevering in them until the end. Be very careful lest, with all our advantages, we fail. Work out your salvation, because it is God who works in you. This encourages us to do our best, because our effort will not be wasted; but we must still depend on God’s grace. The working of God’s grace in us quickens and motivates our efforts. God's kindness toward us is the reason for his work in us. Do your duty without complaining. Do it, and don’t find fault with it. Focus on your work and don’t argue about it. By being peaceful, give no just cause for offense. The children of God should be different from ordinary people. The more unprincipled others are, the more careful we should be to keep ourselves without blame and harmless. The teaching and example of consistent believers will enlighten others and direct their way to Christ and holiness, just as a lighthouse warns sailors of rocks and directs them into harbor. Let us try to shine this way. The gospel is the word of life; it makes known eternal life through Jesus Christ. Running shows earnestness and vigor, continually pressing forward; laboring shows constancy and close application. It is God’s will that believers should rejoice greatly; and those who are fortunate enough to have good ministers have even more reason to rejoice with them.

It is best for us when our duty becomes natural to us—that is, sincerely, not just pretending; with a willing heart and honest intentions. We tend to put our own reputation, comfort, and safety before truth, holiness, and duty, but Timothy did not do so. Paul desired freedom, not that he might enjoy himself, but that he might do good. Epaphroditus was willing to go to the Philippians so that he could be comforted with those who had grieved for him when he was sick. It seems his illness was caused by his work for God. The apostle urges them to love him even more because of this. It is especially pleasant to have our blessings restored by God after a great danger of losing them; and this should make us value them all the more. What is given in answer to prayer should be received with great thankfulness and joy.
